column1: 1,Goroutine Lifecycle,"goroutine, lifecycle, leak, done","Every goroutine you start must have a clear and predictable termination path; otherwise it will leak memory and resources indefinitely.","Always ensure every goroutine has a well-defined exit condition using context cancellation, done channels, or bounded work.","Do not fire off goroutines without knowing when and how they will stop; never assume the garbage collector will clean them up.","func process(ctx context.Context) {

2,Channel Direction,"chan, direction, send, receive, chan<-, <-chan","Using directional channel types in function signatures enforces at compile time whether a function may only send to or only receive from a channel.","Declare channel parameters as chan<- T for send-only or <-chan T for receive-only to restrict access at compile time.","Do not pass bidirectional chan T when the function only needs to send or only needs to receive; you lose compile-time safety.","func producer(out chan<- int) {

column1: 3,Buffered vs Unbuffered,"buffered, unbuffered, channel, capacity","Unbuffered channels synchronize sender and receiver; buffered channels decouple them up to the buffer size, which changes timing and backpressure behavior.","Use unbuffered channels when you need synchronization guarantees; use buffered channels when you understand the required capacity and need to decouple sender/receiver timing.","Do not arbitrarily pick a large buffer size to hide synchronization problems; do not use unbuffered channels when throughput requires decoupling.","func dispatch(jobs []int) <-chan int {

4,Select Default,"select, default, non-blocking, try-send","Adding a default case to a select statement makes channel operations non-blocking, which is useful for try-send and try-receive patterns.","Use select with default when you intentionally want non-blocking channel operations such as try-send or polling loops.","Do not put a default case in select when you actually want to block and wait for channel data; it causes busy spinning.","func trySend(ch chan<- int, val int) bool {

5,Context Cancellation,"context, cancel, timeout, deadline, WithCancel, WithTimeout, WithDeadline","The context package provides WithCancel, WithTimeout, and WithDeadline to propagate cancellation signals through call chains and enforce time bounds on operations.","Always call the returned cancel function using defer; pass context as the first parameter; use WithTimeout or WithDeadline for operations that must not run forever.","Do not ignore the cancel function returned by context constructors; do not store contexts in structs; do not pass nil contexts.","func fetchData(ctx context.Context) error {

column1: 6,errgroup,"errgroup, parallel, error, goroutine","The errgroup package from x/sync runs a group of goroutines and returns the first non-nil error, automatically canceling the group context on failure.","Use errgroup.WithContext to run parallel tasks that share a cancellation scope; check the returned error from g.Wait.","Do not ignore the error from g.Wait; do not use errgroup without context when you need cancellation on first failure.","func fetchAll(ctx context.Context, urls []string) error {

7,WaitGroup,"waitgroup, sync, goroutine, wait","sync.WaitGroup waits for a collection of goroutines to finish; call Add before launching the goroutine, and Done inside the goroutine with defer.","Call wg.Add before launching the goroutine; call wg.Done with defer as the first statement in the goroutine; call wg.Wait to block until all goroutines complete.","Do not call wg.Add inside the goroutine; do not forget to call wg.Done; do not reuse a WaitGroup before Wait returns.","func processItems(items []string) {

column1: 8,sync.Once,"once, singleton, initialization, lazy","sync.Once ensures a function is executed exactly once even when called from multiple goroutines, making it ideal for lazy initialization.","Use sync.Once for one-time initialization of shared resources such as database connections, configuration loading, or singleton instances.","Do not use sync.Once with a function that can fail without providing a retry mechanism; do not put long-blocking work inside the once function as all callers block.","var (

9,Mutex,"mutex, lock, rwmutex, sync, critical section","sync.Mutex provides mutual exclusion for shared mutable state; sync.RWMutex allows concurrent readers with exclusive writers for read-heavy workloads.","Keep the critical section as small as possible; use defer mu.Unlock immediately after Lock; prefer RWMutex when reads vastly outnumber writes.","Do not hold a mutex while performing IO or network calls; do not nest mutex locks without careful ordering to avoid deadlocks; do not copy a mutex after first use.","type SafeCounter struct {

column1: 10,sync.Map,"sync.Map, concurrent, map, thread-safe","sync.Map is optimized for two patterns: keys written once but read many times, and disjoint sets of keys per goroutine; for other patterns a regular map with a mutex is simpler and faster.","Use sync.Map when keys are stable and reads dominate writes, or when goroutines access disjoint key sets; otherwise prefer a regular map guarded by sync.RWMutex.","Do not use sync.Map as a general replacement for map+mutex; do not rely on sync.Map for workloads with heavy concurrent writes to the same keys.","var cache sync.Map

11,Atomic Operations,"atomic, int64, value, load, store, cas","The sync/atomic package provides low-level atomic memory primitives; since Go 1.19 typed wrappers like atomic.Int64 and atomic.Bool provide a safer API.","Use atomic.Int64, atomic.Bool, or atomic.Value for simple counters and flags; prefer higher-level synchronization for complex invariants spanning multiple variables.","Do not use raw atomic.AddInt64 with unaligned fields in structs on 32-bit platforms; do not mix atomic and non-atomic access to the same variable.","type Metrics struct {

12,Fan-out Fan-in,"fan-out, fan-in, distribute, merge, parallel","Fan-out distributes work across multiple goroutines; fan-in collects results from multiple channels into a single channel, enabling parallel processing with a single consumer.","Use fan-out to parallelize CPU-bound or IO-bound work; use fan-in with a WaitGroup to close the merged channel once all producers finish.","Do not forget to close the output channel after all fan-out workers complete; do not create an unbounded number of fan-out goroutines.","func fanOut(in <-chan int, workers int) <-chan int {

13,Worker Pool,"worker, pool, bounded, concurrency, jobs","A worker pool starts a fixed number of goroutines that consume jobs from a shared channel, providing bounded concurrency and controlled resource usage.","Size the pool based on the nature of the work: use runtime.NumCPU for CPU-bound tasks; use higher counts for IO-bound tasks; always close the jobs channel to signal completion.","Do not create one goroutine per task when the number of tasks is unbounded; do not forget to close the jobs channel so workers can exit.","func workerPool(jobs <-chan int, results chan<- int, numWorkers int) {

14,Pipeline,"pipeline, stages, channel, compose","A pipeline is a series of stages connected by channels where each stage is a group of goroutines running the same function, reading from upstream and sending downstream.","Design each stage to accept an input channel and return an output channel; close the output channel when the input is drained; propagate context for cancellation.","Do not skip closing channels between stages; do not ignore context cancellation in pipeline stages; do not create pipelines when a simple loop suffices.","func gen(ctx context.Context, nums ...int) <-chan int {
func square(ctx context.Context, in <-chan int) <-chan int {

column1: 15,Generator,"generator, yield, produce, channel","A generator is a goroutine that produces a sequence of values and sends them on a channel, allowing the caller to consume values lazily via range.","Return a receive-only channel from the generator; close the channel when generation is complete; support context cancellation so the generator can be stopped early.","Do not forget to close the channel in the generator; do not create generators without cancellation support for potentially infinite sequences.","func fibonacci(ctx context.Context, n int) <-chan int {

16,Semaphore,"semaphore, buffered channel, limit, throttle","A buffered channel can act as a counting semaphore to limit the number of concurrent operations by acquiring (sending) and releasing (receiving) tokens.","Use a buffered channel with capacity equal to the desired concurrency limit; acquire a token before starting work and release it when work is done using defer.","Do not forget to release the semaphore token on all code paths; do not use an unbuffered channel as a semaphore; do not set the capacity too high defeating the purpose.","func processWithLimit(ctx context.Context, items []string, limit int) {

17,Pub Sub,"pubsub, publish, subscribe, broadcast, channel","A publish/subscribe system uses channels to broadcast messages from publishers to multiple subscribers, decoupling producers from consumers.","Use a mutex-protected subscriber registry; close or remove subscriber channels on unsubscribe; use non-blocking sends or buffered channels to avoid slow subscribers blocking publishers.","Do not send on a closed subscriber channel; do not let a slow subscriber block all other subscribers; do not forget to unsubscribe to avoid goroutine leaks.","type PubSub struct {

column1: 18,Rate Limiting,"rate, limit, ticker, throttle, time.Ticker","Rate limiting controls how frequently operations are performed using time.Ticker or the x/time/rate package to prevent overwhelming downstream services.","Use time.NewTicker for fixed-rate limiting; use rate.NewLimiter from x/time/rate for token-bucket limiting with burst support; always stop tickers when done.","Do not use time.Sleep in a loop for rate limiting as it does not account for work duration; do not forget to stop the ticker to avoid resource leaks.","func rateLimitedProcess(ctx context.Context, items []string) {

column1: 19,Timeout Pattern,"timeout, time.After, select, deadline","Using select with time.After or context.WithTimeout prevents goroutines from blocking indefinitely on channel operations or long-running tasks.","Use context.WithTimeout for most timeout needs; use select with time.After for simple one-off timeouts; prefer context over raw timers for propagation.","Do not use time.After in a loop as it creates a new timer each iteration that is not garbage collected until it fires; use time.NewTimer and reset it instead.","func queryWithTimeout(ctx context.Context) (string, error) {

column1: 20,Graceful Shutdown,"shutdown, signal, os.Signal, interrupt, SIGTERM","Graceful shutdown listens for OS termination signals and uses context cancellation to stop all goroutines cleanly before the process exits.","Use signal.NotifyContext or signal.Notify to catch SIGINT/SIGTERM; cancel a root context to propagate shutdown; use a timeout to force exit if cleanup takes too long.","Do not call os.Exit directly without cleaning up resources; do not ignore the second signal which should force an immediate exit.","func main() {

21,Channel Closing Rules,"close, channel, sender, receiver, panic","Only the sender should close a channel, never the receiver; closing a channel signals to all receivers that no more values will be sent.","Close channels from the sender side only; use sync.Once or a dedicated goroutine to ensure a channel is closed exactly once; use the comma-ok idiom to detect closed channels.","Do not close a channel from the receiver side; do not close a channel more than once; do not send on a closed channel as it panics.","func produce(items []int) <-chan int {

column1: 22,Goroutine Leak Detection,"goleak, leak, testing, goroutine","The go.uber.org/goleak package detects goroutine leaks in tests by verifying that no unexpected goroutines remain after a test completes.","Use goleak.VerifyNone in tests to catch goroutine leaks; run it at the end of each test or as a TestMain wrapper for the whole package.","Do not ignore goroutine leak test failures; do not skip goleak checks in tests that launch goroutines; do not filter out leaked goroutines without investigation.","func TestNoLeak(t *testing.T) {

column1: 23,Singleflight,"singleflight, dedup, cache, concurrent","The singleflight package deduplicates concurrent function calls so that only one execution occurs for a given key, and all callers share the result.","Use singleflight.Group.Do to deduplicate cache fills, database queries, or API calls that multiple goroutines request simultaneously with the same key.","Do not use singleflight for operations with side effects that must execute per caller; do not cache the singleflight.Group in a way that prevents garbage collection.","var group singleflight.Group

column1: 24,Bounded Concurrency,"bounded, concurrency, limit, semaphore, pool","Bounded concurrency limits the number of goroutines running simultaneously to prevent resource exhaustion using semaphores, worker pools, or errgroup.SetLimit.","Use errgroup with SetLimit, a semaphore channel, or a fixed worker pool to bound the number of concurrent goroutines to a sensible limit.","Do not launch one goroutine per item in an unbounded collection; do not rely on the runtime scheduler to handle thousands of goroutines performing IO.","func processItems(ctx context.Context, items []string) error {

column1: 25,Done Channel,"done, channel, signal, completion, close","A done channel is a channel of empty structs used purely for signaling completion or cancellation; closing it broadcasts the signal to all listeners simultaneously.","Use chan struct{} for done channels; close the channel to broadcast the signal; select on the done channel alongside other operations for cancellation.","Do not send a value on the done channel instead of closing it as that only signals one listener; do not use chan bool when no data needs to be transmitted.","func worker(done <-chan struct{}, tasks <-chan int) {
